relationships.history: Met on a family court consult. Paloma counted the repairs. Marcus named what was being protested. Together they wrote a single-page summary the judge actually read.
relationships.current_tension: Paloma wants to start with the sequence. Marcus wants to start with the feeling. They have learned to alternate.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They once agreed a transcript was functionally a breakup letter, neither speaker had noticed yet.

relationships.history: Paloma trained Iris's unit in conversation analysis for one week in 2015. Iris kept the handout and still refers to it.
relationships.current_tension: Paloma reads for mutual accomplishment. Iris reads for adversarial leakage. Their observations often agree and their interpretations rarely do.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both distrust clinicians who paraphrase transcripts. They do not say this in front of the clinicians.

relationships.history: Debated on a podcast for three hours about whether subtext is real. They did not resolve it. Listeners wrote in for weeks.
relationships.current_tension: Paloma says the conversation does its own work on the surface. Evelyn says the surface is the least interesting part. Neither will yield.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They respect each other more than their public disagreements would suggest.

relationships.history: Worked a family mediation together. Paloma mapped the turn-taking. Sam mapped the triangle. The diagrams overlaid almost exactly.
relationships.current_tension: Almost none. They share a skepticism of individual diagnosis and a love for the pattern that sits above the speakers.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both suspect the person not in the transcript is often the most important speaker.

relationships.history: Co-authored a paper on markers of coerced confession. Paloma supplied the sequence analysis. Henrik supplied the lexical work. Cited heavily in training materials.
relationships.current_tension: They finish each other's sentences. It unnerves the rest of the room.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both believe most clinicians are bad at reading transcripts and should be humbler about it.

relationships.history: Lena took one of Paloma's workshops and tells people it changed how she heard her clients. Paloma is quietly flattered.
relationships.current_tension: Lena wants to move fast to the affective read. Paloma wants to slow down and look at the turn. They have learned to let Paloma go first.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They agree that bids for connection are often invisible until you look at what the other person did with them.

relationships.history: Consulted together on a labor negotiation. Paloma found the point where the union lost control of the sequence. Theo found the point where they lost the thread of their own motivation. Both reads mattered.
relationships.current_tension: Theo is patient with ambivalence. Paloma is patient with structure. They slow each other down in useful ways.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both refuse to tell the client what to do, which infuriates clients and produces better outcomes.

relationships.history: Worked a threat assessment together. Marcus saw an attachment rupture. Iris saw a credible plan. They were both correct. It was uncomfortable.
relationships.current_tension: Marcus reads for pain. Iris reads for pattern. They rarely land in the same place and they have stopped trying to pretend otherwise.
relationships.shared_knowledge: Iris thinks Marcus is naive. Marcus thinks Iris is cold. Both think the other is sometimes indispensable.

relationships.history: Trained at adjacent institutes in the 1990s. Have been trading cases for twenty-five years. Evelyn is the person Marcus calls when he cannot see his own countertransference.
relationships.current_tension: Marcus reads attachment. Evelyn reads enactment. They overlap more than they diverge, and when they diverge Evelyn usually sees it first.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They know each other's analysts. They do not discuss this in professional contexts.

relationships.history: Co-led a training on couples work embedded in family systems. Marcus drew the attachment diagrams. Sam drew the triangles. The training is in its eighth year.
relationships.current_tension: Marcus asks what the two people are protecting. Sam asks who the third person is. Neither question invalidates the other.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They agree that a couple is almost never a closed system. Marcus frames it as history. Sam frames it as present field.

relationships.history: Met exactly once before joining this consortium, at a conference panel where they disagreed civilly about whether a transcript could reveal interior states.
relationships.current_tension: Marcus trusts resonance. Henrik trusts measurement. They respect each other at arm's length.
relationships.shared_knowledge: Henrik has admitted privately that Marcus's readings are sometimes correct in ways his data cannot explain. He does not like this.

relationships.history: Run a couples intensive together every summer. Marcus teaches attachment. Lena teaches the Gottman frame. Students say the weekend changes them.
relationships.current_tension: Lena calls contempt fast. Marcus names the fear under it. They have learned to do both in sequence.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both know Lena's divorce and Marcus's two divorces have made them better clinicians. They sometimes joke about this, when nobody else can hear.

relationships.history: Theo trained at Marcus's institute. Marcus was on his dissertation committee. They now consult on each other's hardest cases.
relationships.current_tension: Marcus reads for longing. Theo reads for ambivalence. They agree that both are forms of the same ache.
relationships.shared_knowledge: Theo once asked Marcus if attachment theory and motivational interviewing were saying the same thing in different languages. Marcus has thought about this for years and is not sure.

relationships.history: Evelyn once spent an afternoon interviewing Iris for a piece on pattern recognition and countertransference. The piece never ran. Evelyn still has the notes.
relationships.current_tension: Iris thinks Evelyn reads too much into things. Evelyn thinks Iris reads too little. They circle each other with more interest than either will admit.
relationships.shared_knowledge: Evelyn can sometimes predict what Iris will flag. Iris pretends not to notice.

relationships.history: Consulted together on a workplace harassment case. Iris found the deceptive speaker. Sam found the enabling system. Both reports were necessary.
relationships.current_tension: Iris wants to pin the bad actor. Sam wants to map the field that produced the bad actor. They have made peace with doing both.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both think pure individual explanations of bad behavior are lazy, for different reasons.

relationships.history: Worked a dozen cases together for the Bureau. Iris does behavioral. Henrik does linguistic. Their combined reports are considered the gold standard by the people who read them.
relationships.current_tension: They argue about exactly which signals are diagnostic. The arguments are friendly and productive.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both keep a list of cases they got wrong. Each has shown the other. Neither has shown anyone else.

relationships.history: Lena once sat in on one of Iris's trainings to see how law enforcement reads couples conflict. She took a lot of notes and none of the techniques.
relationships.current_tension: Iris is unsparing. Lena is warm. They do not share clients and they do not pretend they would.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They agree that contempt is a reliable tell, for different reasons.

relationships.history: Consulted together on a negotiation where one party was possibly manipulating the other. Iris flagged the deception. Theo flagged the ambivalence of the target. Both were right.
relationships.current_tension: Iris wants to call the behavior. Theo wants to stay curious about it. They have learned to let Theo go first with subjects and Iris go first with facts.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both think premature interpretation is the biggest mistake in any reading.

relationships.history: Evelyn invited Sam to lecture at her institute on how systems ideas fit inside an analytic frame. The lecture was so well-received Sam now teaches it annually.
relationships.current_tension: Evelyn works with inner worlds. Sam works with outer systems. They agree more than they disagree, which both find faintly uncanny.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both believe most transcripts are more about absent people than present ones.

relationships.history: Debated at a symposium on evidence and interpretation. Evelyn enjoyed herself enormously. Henrik did not.
relationships.current_tension: Henrik thinks Evelyn is unfalsifiable. Evelyn thinks Henrik is missing almost everything that matters. Neither is going to change.
relationships.shared_knowledge: Henrik has quietly shown one of Evelyn's case readings to his students as an example of the kind of interpretation one should not make. She knows.

relationships.history: Lena was supervised briefly by Evelyn during training. She calls her 'Dr. Crane' in public and 'Evelyn' in private. Evelyn remembers every case they ever discussed.
relationships.current_tension: Lena works on the surface of the couple. Evelyn works under it. They trade cases often.
relationships.shared_knowledge: Evelyn once told Lena that contempt is a disavowal of one's own dependency. Lena has never forgotten it.

relationships.history: Theo reads Evelyn's books. Evelyn has read exactly one of Theo's papers and pretends she has read more. Theo is not fooled and does not mind.
relationships.current_tension: Evelyn goes for the deep interpretation. Theo goes for the collaborative next question. They appreciate each other.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both think the most interesting thing in any transcript is the contradiction a speaker does not yet know they are holding.

relationships.history: Have worked on two projects together. Both went fine. Neither has sought a third.
relationships.current_tension: Sam works in relationships. Henrik works in syntax. They have very little to say to each other and they are both at peace with that.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They respect each other professionally and would not pick each other for a drink.

relationships.history: Met in a peer supervision group twelve years ago. Have been calling each other about cases ever since. Their kids know each other.
relationships.current_tension: Sam pulls out to the system. Lena zooms in to the bid. They have learned to do both, and they have also learned when to stop each other.
relationships.shared_knowledge: Lena was the first person Sam told about his brother's cutoff. She is one of two people who know.

relationships.history: Shared a case in a community clinic early in both their careers. Neither could fix it alone. Together they did not fix it either, but they understood it better.
relationships.current_tension: Theo is patient with individual ambivalence. Sam is patient with whole-system stuckness. They remind each other not to rush.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both believe change is almost always slower than the client or the clinician would prefer, and that this is almost always fine.

relationships.history: Have attended two of the same conferences. Had a friendly lunch once where they mostly talked about food.
relationships.current_tension: Henrik finds emotion unquantifiable. Lena finds pure lexical analysis dry. They do not mix much.
relationships.shared_knowledge: Lena has asked Henrik specific questions about word frequency in couples conflict. He has answered precisely. She has used it.

relationships.history: Had a long conversation on a hotel patio after a conference panel. Turned out they both played the same board game. Became unlikely correspondents.
relationships.current_tension: They disagree on almost everything substantive. They enjoy each other anyway.
relationships.shared_knowledge: Henrik once admitted to Theo that uncertainty felt unbearable to him. Theo said 'so does certainty, to me.' They left it there.

relationships.history: Interned in the same clinic eighteen years ago. Have kept up ever since. Each was a guest at the other's wedding. Lena's did not last. Theo's did.
relationships.current_tension: Lena names dynamics fast. Theo sits with them slow. They think they balance each other and they are probably right.
relationships.shared_knowledge: They both think Marcus is wiser than he gives himself credit for. They also both think Evelyn is sometimes showing off. They tell no one but each other.
